title: Wet-felted clutch bag
subtitle: A small envelope-shaped bag felted over a plastic resist, finished with a snap fastener
excerpt: Felt an envelope-shaped bag from merino roving using a flat plastic resist, then trim the opening to a neat edge and attach a magnetic snap fastener. The clutch is approximately 22 cm wide by 14 cm tall when finished. The technique is the same resist-felting method used for slippers and vessels, applied to a flat pouch shape.
sourceNotes: Resist-felted bags are a standard project in advanced wet-felting workshops. The envelope form is among the simplest bag shapes for the method.

content.text: for this bag is a rectangle 32 cm wide by 20 cm tall cut from bubble wrap or 3 mm craft foam. The finished bag measures approximately 22 x 14 cm after 30% shrinkage. The bag reaches
stage before the resist is removed, then fulls to final density on the mat without the resist inside.

items.name: Merino roving, contrast colour (optional stripe or edge)
Bubble wrap or 3 mm craft foam (for resist)
Hot water and cold water access

content.text: Place the resist on the mat. Lay three cross-hatched layers of merino over the resist, leaving 2 cm overhang on the two long sides and the rounded base. Leave the top edge (the bag opening) without overhang. Wet out through the net with hot soapy water. Rub through the net with flat palms until the fibres begin to lock.
Step 2: Turn and lay the second face

Flip the resist over. Fold the side and base overhangs onto this face. Lay three cross-hatched layers on top. Wet out and rub as before. After rubbing, roll the piece tightly in the mat and roll 40 passes. Unroll, check for even shrinkage beginning, and repeat.
Step 3: Remove the resist and full

When the bag has shrunk by about a third and the fabric holds its shape, cut a small slit at the opening and ease the resist out. Squeeze out excess water. Continue rolling and fulling the empty bag until the walls are dense and firm. Throw the bag against the mat surface a few times; this drives the final shrinkage quickly.

While damp, shape the bag by hand to straighten the sides and even the base. Trim the opening edge to a clean straight line with scissors. Rinse in cold water and dry flat, stuffed with crumpled newspaper or a folded cloth to hold the shape. Once dry, sew the magnetic snap at the opening centre, reinforcing each snap plate with a small square of wool fabric on the inside.

items.fix: When laying, pay extra attention to corners; press additional wisps of roving at each corner point.
items.cause: Roving did not cover the corners of the resist at full thickness
items.problem: The bag base corners are thin and almost transparent

items.fix: Cut the resist to extend to the very edge of the intended base; the resist prevents fusion, so it must cover the full base area.
items.cause: The resist was too small and the overhang fused front to back at the base
items.problem: The inside of the bag is rough and felted to itself at the base

items.fix: Stuff and shape while wet, then pin the opening edge flat against a foam board while drying.
items.cause: The bag dried unstuffed, allowing the opening to curl
items.problem: The opening edge is uneven and wavy
